params.description: Dates value (60) highlighted in data table.

steps.text: Scale of 2 works, but look at the biggest number: 60. That means 30 tick marks on the axis. With Scale of 10, you only need 6 tick marks. Which is easier to read?

params.description: Scale of 5 and Scale of 10 options highlighted side by side.

steps.text: Scale of 5 fits all the data. But look: to reach 60, you need 12 tick marks. Scale of 10 only needs 6. Both work, but which graph is cleaner?

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: System demonstrates Scale of 10. Axis shows 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60. All bars fit cleanly: Dates at 60, Cherries at 45, Bananas at 30, Apples at 15.

steps.text: Here's how I think about it. The biggest number is 60. All values end in 0 or 5, so Scale of 5 and Scale of 10 both show exact values. But Scale of 10 only needs 6 tick marks while Scale of 5 needs 12. Fewer marks make the graph easier to read. The answer is Scale of 10 because it balances precision with clarity.

params.description: System highlights each reason in sequence: ones place digits showing 0 or 5, axis with only 6 clean tick marks, all bars fitting within range, comparison showing Scale of 2 would need 30 marks.

steps.text: Let me walk through why Scale of 10 works. First, all values end in 0 or 5, so they land exactly on tick marks or halfway between. Second, the graph is easy to read with only 6 marks on the axis. Third, the biggest number is 60, so all bars fit well. Fourth, Scale of 2 would need 30 marks to reach 60, which is too many. All four reasons explain why Scale of 10 is the best choice here.
